title: Whoopi Goldberg Slams Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do You Care Who’s Cast in It?’
description:
Jul 23, 2026 3:07am PT	
	

	
		Whoopi Goldberg Slams Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do
body:
Jul 23, 2026 3:07am PT	
	

	
		Whoopi Goldberg Slams Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do You Care Who’s Cast in It?’
	
	
			


			
			
	

									Getty Images
	

		
	
				
	Whoopi Goldberg used Tuesday’s episode of “The View” to push back on conservative anger over Elliot Page‘s casting in Christopher Nolan‘s “The Odyssey,” dismissing a clip of commentator Ben Shapiro calling the choice a mistake.

	Shapiro called Page’s role an “absolute puzzler” before pointing out that he is “about 5’2 and 105 pounds soaking wet and has the voice of a woman. It doesn’t work.”


	“A lot of conservatives who see this are going to feel the same way. That’s okay. But I don’t understand. Why go see the movie? If you don’t want to see it, don’t go see it. Why do you care who’s cast in it?” Goldberg said.
	

	Page has a supporting role in the film as Sinon, a cousin of Matt Damon’s Odysseus. The Variety review described Page’s role as “tersely vulnerable.”

	Goldberg didn’t stop at defending Page. She also went after the word “woke” itself, telling viewers that the label gets thrown at ordinary people just trying to hold down jobs, raise families and get an education, not at anyone pushing an agenda.

	Critics on the right had first seized on inaccurate rumors that Page would be cast as Achilles, with Elon Musk among those weighing in before the actual casting was known. A similarly hostile response met Lupita Nyong’o’s involvement in the project, rooted in racist objections to her casting.

	The criticism has had zero effect on box office for “The Odyssey.” Nolan enjoyed the biggest opening of his career with the film, with the global collections now having crossed $300 million. 


	
		More from Variety

schema.headline: Whoopi Goldberg Slams Critics of Elliot Page’s Casting in ‘The Odyssey’: ‘Then Don’t Go See It… Why Do You Care Who’s Cast in It?’

title: Venice Film Festival Reveals 2026 Lineup (Updating Live)
description:
The Venice Film Festival is about to reveal its 2026 lineup, promising a plethora of buzzy titles from international directors.

	Like Cannes this y
body:
The Venice Film Festival is about to reveal its 2026 lineup, promising a plethora of buzzy titles from international directors.

	Like Cannes this year, Venice is expected to have a lighter Hollywood presence than in previous editions. But that does not mean there won’t be a robust roster of awards contenders.

	As previously announced, the festival will open with the world premiere of Danny Boyles “Ink,” which will also compete for the festival’s prestigious Golden Lion. The 1960s-set biographical drama stars Guy Pearce as divisive business magnate Rupert Murdoch and Jack O’Connell as Larry Lamb, the editor of Murdoch-owned U.K. tabloid The Sun. Netflix recently acquired the U.S. rights to the film. 
	

	Closing Venice this year is “Dio Ride,” the latest work from Italian filmmaker Giovanni Veronesi, in the out of competition section. The film, whose title translates to “God Laughs,” stars Pierfrancesco Favino (“Maria,” “World War Z”) as a friar in 17th-century Tuscany who manages to speak about God in a more joyful way. However, his light-hearted approach provokes the hostility of the church.


	“The Bride” and “The Lost Daughter” director Maggie Gyllenhaal is set to preside over the competition jury, the third woman to do so in the past five years following Isabelle Huppert, Julianne Moore and Cate Blanchett. She will be joined by Hong Kong filmmaking giant Johnnie To, Tunisian director Kaouther Ben Hania, Afghan filmmaker Shahrbanoo Sadat, French director and screenwriter Xavier Giannoli, “The Brutalist” composer Daniel Blumberg and U.S.-based Italian film academic Francesco Casetti. 
	

	George Clooney and Ellen Burstyn will be honored with lifetime achievement awards.

	The 83rd Venice Film Festival runs from Sept. 2 to 12. 

	See the full lineup below, updating live.

	COMPETITION

	“Ink,” Danny Boyle (opening film)

	VENICE OPEN (OUT OF COMPETITION — FICTION)

	“Dio Ride,” Giovanni Veronesi (closing film)

	OUT OF COMPETITION — NON-FICTION

	“Be Brave,” Francesco Carrozzini

	“What Love Builds,” Russell Crowe

	“Burgundy,” Michael Dweck, Gregory Kershaw

	“Musk,” Alex Gibney

	“The Road to Jericho,” Amos Gitai

	“Al Mowaten Osama” (“Citizen Osama”), Ahmed Hassaouna

	“Union Town,” Barbara Kopple

	“Holy Wood Dust,” Victor Kossakovsky

	“Listy” (“Letters From the Silenced Country”), Andrei Kutsila

	“Biografía De Jorge Luis Borges,” Mariano Llinás

	“My Undesirable Friends: Part II – Exile,” Julia Loktev

	“Boatbuilders,” Luke Lorentzen

	“Imperium,” Sergei Loznitsa

	“Shumei – The Living Legacy of Kabuki,” Takashi Miike

	“Queer Edward II,” Theo Rollason

	“Oasis: Don’t Look Back in Anger,” Dylan Southern, Will Lovelace

	SPECIAL SCREENING

	“Dai Nostri Inviati. La Rai Racconta La Mostra del Cinema di Venezia 2001-2012,” Giuseppe Giannotti, Enrico Salvatori, Nicoletta Berardi

	FILM ON FILMS

	“Juso Per Ferie,” Karen Di Porto

	“Nino – Un Film Su Nino Rota,” Walter Fasano

	“The Pervert’s Guide to Utopias,” Sophie Fiennes

	“Joie De Vivre,” Luca Guadagnino

	“Twist and Shoot Mister Suzuki,” Yves Montmayeur

	“Intermission,” Yonfan

	OUT OF COMPETITION — SHORTS

	“Flesh Impact,” Maggie Gyllenhaal

	“Halayon” (“Wild Asparagus”), Avi Mograbi

	“They’re Here,” Laura Poitras, Rachel Mueller

	OUT OF COMPETITION — SERIES

	“Peccato,” Valerio Vestoso

	HORIZONS

	“La Ragazza Con La Leica,” Alina Marazzi (opening film)

	“Yak Mai” (“The Burning Giants”), Phuttiphong Aroonpheng

	“Cudze Rzeczy” (“What Belongs to Others”), Grzegorz Dębowski

	“Una Storia,” Anna Foglietta

	“La Città Dei Vivi,” Edoardo Gabriellini

	“Moragheb” (“Falling House”), Mohsen Gharaei

	“Too Much Sugar,” Robert Greene
	

	“The Difficult Bride,” Rubaiyat Hossain

	“La Maison Du Vent,” Auguste Bernard Kouemo Yanghu

	“I Figli Della Scimmia,” Tommaso Landucci

	“Mia Mera Sti Zoi Tis Tzo: Kefalaio Faidra” (“A Day in the Life of Jo: Chapter Phaedra”), Jaqueline Lentzou

	“Oase” (“Oasis”), Jannis Lenz

	“Do Kraja Dana” (“Until the Day Ends”), Jelena Maksimović

	“La Moula,” Jérôme Pierrat

	“Lovers in the Blue Night,” Anuparna Roy

	“Un Détour Par Diane,” Ann Sirot, Raphaël Balboni

	“The Color of the Sun,” Xavier Tera

	“Eklipse,” Manuel Wetscher

	“Huangyan Shenghuo” (“Big Little Things”), Michelle Zhou

	VENICE SPOTLIGHT

	“Sumo: Spirit Weighs Nothing,” Erik Shirai 

	“Hiwar Ma’ Albahr” (“Conversation With the Sea”), Muayad Alayan

	“Hombre Al Agua,” Gael García Bernal

	“Serpenti,” Roberto De Paolis Maino

	“Furies,” Rami Kodeih

	“Guria,” Levan Koguashvili

	“Földszint” (“Ground Floor”), Gábor Reisz

	“Eu Contez” (“I Matter”), Alina Șerban

	Elsa Keslassy contributed to this report.

title: Luiz Carlos Barreto, Its Legendary Film Producer, Dies at 98
description: Luiz Carlos Barreto, one of the driving forces of the Cinema Novo movement in the 1960s and 1970s, died in Rio de Janeiro on Wednesday, July 22, at
body:
Luiz Carlos Barreto, one of the driving forces of the Cinema Novo movement in the 1960s and 1970s, died in Rio de Janeiro on Wednesday, July 22, at the age of 98.

	In reaction – and it’s a measure of the huge regard with which Barreto was held in his native Brazil and beyond – Brazilian President Lula Inácio Lula da Silva declared July 22 three days of national mourning in his honor, which means the national flag will be flown at half-mast. 
	

	Barreto’s health has been in frail condition since March 2024, when he suffered a fall. The official cause of death is pneumonia.


	Barreto is regarded as one of the most important figures in the history of Brazilian cinema, with a career spanning six decades. He was involved in more than 80 productions, and was closely associated with the avant-garde Cinema Novo movement, which rocked Cannes and beyond in the 196os and early 1970s, signalling a future for cinema, in the eyes of French Nouvelle Vague leading lights.

	Born in 1928 in the Northeastern Ceará State, Barreto moved to Rio de Janeiro in 1947 at the age of 19, and worked as a photographer for the important newsmagazine O Cruzeiro. At an interview in 1952, he met Lucy Vilella, then beauty queen of Miss Goias State and a notable pianist. The two got married two years later, and became lifetime partners, including in film production.

	In further work as a photographer in 1961, Barreto visited in Bahia the set of “Barravento” the first feature of Glauber Rocha, who would become the leading figure of Cinema Novo. Rocha encouraged Barreto to make his debut as co-screenwriter and co-producer of Roberto Farias’ “Assalto ao Trem Pagador” (1962). Next, he worked as the cinematographer on Nelson Pereira dos Santos’ classic “Barren Lives” (Vidas Secas) (1963).
	

	Barreto founded in 1963 L.C. Barreto Produções Cinematográficas, the longest-running film production company in Brazil. Together with Lucy Barreto, they made over 80 features, which take in many of the great films of the Cinema Novo such as Joaquim Pedro de Andrade’s “Garrincha: Joy of the People” (Garrincha, Alegria do Povo) (1963), Roberto Santos’ “The Hour and Turn of Augusto Matraga” (“A Hora e a Vez de Augusto Matraga”) (1965), Joaquim Pedro de Andrade’s “The Priest and the Girl” (“O Padre e a Moça”) (1966), Glauber Rocha’s “Entranced Earth” (“Terra em Transe”) (1967) and “Antonio das Mortes” (“O Dragão da Maldade contra o Santo Guerreiro”) (1969) and Nelson Pereira dos Santos’ “How Tasty Was My Little Frenchman” (“Como Era Gostoso o Meu Francês”) (1971).

	As Brazil’s Cinema Novo disolved under military dictatorship, L.C. Barreto still managed to make films, such as Cacá Diegues’ main Cannes competition contender “Bye Bye Brazil” (“Bye Bye Brasil”) (1979) and Nelson Pereira dos Santos’ “Memoirs of Prison” (Memórias do Cárcere) (1984).

	The three children of Luiz Carlos and Lucy Barreto inherited the passion for cinema. Bruno Barreto is a renowned director, with features such as classic “Dona Flor and Her Two Husbands” (Dona Flor e Seus Dois Maridos) (1976), one of the biggest box office hits in Brazil, and “Four Days in September” (“O Que É Isso, Companheiro?”) (1997), nominated for a then Foreign Language Oscar. He also had a career in Hollywood. Fábio Barreto, who died in 2019, helmed “O Quatrilho” (1995), also a Foreign Language Oscar-nominee, and “Lula, the Son of Brazil” (“Lula, o Filho do Brasil”) (2009). Producer Paula now serves as CEO of L.C. Barreto, still a leading production company in Brazil.

	“The greatest legacy my father leaves me is the person he was. He used to say, ‘My party is the party of Brazilian cinema.’ He always opened channels of dialogue in support of Brazilian cinema,” Paula Barreto told Variety. 

	She added: “When ‘Dona Flor’ was being butchered by the censors [during the dictatorship in Brazil], he got on a plane, spent a week in Brasilia, and convinced the [dictator Ernesto] Geisel’s wife and daughter to watch the film and defend it.”
	

	In a social media post, Lula highlighted Barreto’s importance to Brazilian culture, announcing the official three-day mourning period in recognition of the filmmaker’s contribution: “Brazilian cinema owes a great deal to Luiz Carlos Barreto, who left us this Wednesday. His love for Brazil, his talent and, above all, his restless spirit made him the most important producer in Brazilian cinema over the last 50 years.”

	Luiz Carlos Barreto is survived by Lucy Barreto; his children, Paula and Bruno, and nine grandchildren: Julia, Mariana, Lucas and João, Fábio’s children; Helena, Olympia and Gabriel, Bruno’s children; and Camilla and Felipe, Paula’s children. Most of his grandchildren have at some point worked at LC Barreto.

schema.headline: Luiz Carlos Barreto, Its Legendary Film Producer, Dies at 98

title: Sarajevo Film Festival Unveils Competition Programs With 21 World Premieres, Six International Premieres
description: The Sarajevo Film Festival, the leading festival for South-East European cinema, unveiled the lineups for the four competition sections on Thursday,
body:
The Sarajevo Film Festival, the leading festival for South-East European cinema, unveiled the lineups for the four competition sections on Thursday, which include 21 world premieres, six international premieres, one European premiere and 23 regional premieres. The 32nd edition runs from Aug. 14 to 21.

	The narrative feature films section includes nine films from South-East Europe, of which one film will have its world premiere, one its international premiere, one its European premiere, and six their regional premieres.
	

	Elma Tataragić, the programmer of the section, said, “The selection is dominated by second and third features, alongside one debut and two fifth feature films, reflecting both the emergence of new voices and the continued development of established auteurs. Together, these nine films offer a rich panorama of contemporary regional cinema, spanning intimate family dramas, coming-of-age stories, social realism, and formally adventurous works. With six of the nine films directed by women, this year’s competition reflects not only the artistic diversity of the region but also the growing strength and plurality of voices shaping its cinematic future.” 


	Competition Program – Narrative Feature Film:EVERYTHING THAT’S WRONG WITH YOU, Urša Menart (Slovenia, Germany, Serbia, 2026, 97 min.) – World PremiereAfter the emotional collapse that follows her mother’s death, Maruša builds an online bond with Alja, a nurse living in a small coastal town in Germany. Bonding through humor over their shared struggles, Maruša and Alja quickly develop a friendship that is put to the test when they finally meet in person.SMALL THINGS, Zvonimir Jurić (Croatia, Slovenia, Serbia, 2026, 80 min.) – International PremiereDuring a winter weekend, sisters Anica and Irena, accompanied by Irena’s two children, travel to an island to visit their father and their brother Ivan, a quiet loner. The reason for the gathering is their father’s decision to sell the family land. Confined in the worn-down family house, the members of the family are forced to confront long-suppressed tensions and difficult choices, while one of them will have to make the ultimate sacrifice.SKATEBOARDING IS NOT FOR GIRLS, Dina Duma (North Macedonia, Belgium, Slovenia, Croatia, 2026, 82 min.) – European PremiereIn Skopje, 11-year-old Adela and her elder sister Zara share a deep bond shaped by their navigation of two contradictory worlds. At school, they are typical, rebellious teens with skateboards, social media, crop tops, and boyfriends. After school, in a quiet and disciplined mode, they help their mother with cleaning jobs to make ends meet. When poverty shakes the family, Adela tries to find a way to save her sister – still a minor – from a fate that is becoming almost inevitable: an arranged marriage.17, Kosara Mitić (North Macedonia, Serbia, Slovenia, 2026, 105 min.) – Regional PremiereSeventeen-year-old Sara is hiding a secret during a school trip. When the excursion spirals out of control and Sara witnesses her friend Lina being sexually assaulted, the two girls try to break the cycle of casual violence. This seals an everlasting bond between them.DUA, Blerta Basholli (Kosovo, Switzerland, France, 2026, 101 min.) – Regional PremierePristina, Kosovo, late 1990s. As war looms and ethnic tensions escalate, 13-year-old Dua struggles to find her place among her peers and within her changing body. After an incident shakes her community, she becomes a target herself and bonds with a fearless girl who draws her toward an unexpected form of resistance. Between the daily violence and the growing threat of exile, there is little room for quiet awakenings.EVERYTIME, Sandra Wollner (Austria, Germany, 2026, 121 min.) – Regional PremiereA tragedy brings a mother, a daughter, and a teenage boy together. Struggling with blame and forgiveness, the unlikely trio takes a trip to Tenerife for a family holiday that never happens. Under the glow of the sun, past and present quietly start to overlap.SHAME AND MONEY, Visar Morina (Germany, Kosovo, Slovenia, Albania, North Macedonia, Belgium, 2026, 129 min.) – Regional PremiereA couple in their forties live with their extended family in rural Kosovo, where they make a modest living from their farm. Following a familial dispute, their livelihood is lost. They are forced to move to Pristina, where the pressure to provide for their family weighs heavily on the parents as they do their best to find work and a place to stay. A journey into a hyper-capitalist society begins.SUPPORTING ROLE, Ana Urushadze (Georgia, Estonia, Türkiye, 2026, 139 min.) – Regional PremiereNiaz Ninua has seen brighter days. Once a leading man, he stepped away from cinema some 15 years ago. When he decides to return, he discovers a landscape that no longer revolves around him. He attends a casting session for a supporting role in a debut feature directed by a young woman. The encounter bruises his pride, and he leaves unconvinced, dismissing the role as pretentious. Yet, as he replays the encounter, Niaz begins to sense a pull toward the role he so quickly dismissed. What seemed beneath him starts to reveal contours he did not anticipate.YOU DON’T BELONG HERE, Florin Șerban (Romania, 2026, 154 min.) – Regional PremiereMarius, the chief medical officer at a hospital in his small hometown, is raising his teenage son Ioan alone after the death of his wife. Marius is a confident professional who has no idea what his son gets up to at night: cycling through town vandalizing cars with a gang of masked vigilantes. Marius’ world is shaken when he learns the police have come to suspect his son of murder.
	

	Competition Program – Documentary Feature:BEAR WITH ME, Martin Genovski (Bulgaria, 2026, 84 min.) – World PremiereGOOD PEOPLE, Milcho Manchevski (North Macedonia, 2026, 86 min.) – World PremiereTHE BALKAN COWBOY, Dragana Jurišić (Croatia, Ireland, 2026, 93 min.) – World PremiereTHE FORBIDDEN AUNT, Bojana Novaković (Serbia, Australia, 2026, 82 min.) – World PremiereWE SHALL LIVE TOGETHER, Damir Markovina (Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, 2026, 75 min.) – World PremiereDON’T ASK ME IF I KILLED, Helena Maksyom (Romania, Netherlands, Germany, Ukraine, 2026, 103 min.) – International PremiereFRAGMENTS OF BELONGING, Tatjana Božić (Croatia, Netherlands, Germany, 2026, 80 min.) – International PremiereWHEN I GET OUT, Metod Pevec (Slovenia, 2026, 106 min.) – International Premiere32 METERS, Morteza Atabaki (Türkiye, Iran, Qatar, 2025, 83 min.) – Regional PremiereBUGBOY, Lucas Paleocrassas (Greece, France, Denmark, 2026, 87 min.) – Regional PremiereELECTING MS SANTA, Raisa Răzmeriță (Moldova, Romania, 2025, 95 min.) – Regional PremiereFOR LIFE, Ahmet Seven (Türkiye, 2026, 89 min.) – Regional PremiereTO HOLD A MOUNTAIN, Biljana Tutorov, Petar Glomazić (Serbia, France, Montenegro, Slovenia, Croatia, 2026, 105 min.) – Regional Premiere

	Competition Program – Documentary Shorts:BRO RAM, Branko Schmidt (Croatia, 2026, 14 min.) – World PremiereFAHRUDIN, Alen Drljević (Bosnia and Herzegovina, 2026, 20 min.) – World PremiereOF LIGHT GHOSTS, Isabella Fellinger (Austria, 2026, 27 min.) – World PremiereTHE CAPTAIN AND THE CASPIAN SEA, Turkan Huseyin (Azerbaijan, 2026, 30 min.) – World PremiereDEAR ORCHID, Hana Selena Sokolović (Bosnia and Herzegovina, Croatia, Serbia, Netherlands, 2026, 18 min.) – International PremiereASPARAGUS BEAR, Ivan Grgur (Croatia, 2025, 18 min.) – Regional PremiereHOLY DEFIANCE, Karla Jelić (Croatia, 2026, 15 min.) – Regional PremiereTHE FERRY FLOWS, Zhora Papoian (Ukraine, United Kingdom, 2026, 18 min.) – Regional Premiere

	Out of CompetitionYUGO GOES TO AMERICA, Filip Grujić, Aleksa Borković (Serbia, Croatia, 2026, 87 min.) – National Premiere

	Competition Program – Narrative Short FilmDITCH, Marianna Vas (Hungary, 2026, 24 min.) – World PremiereHIDDEN IN FRAME, Anna Sarukhanova (Georgia, United Kingdom, 2026, 16 min.) – World PremiereMAMAMA, Danica Dakić (Bosnia and Herzegovina, 2026, 8 min.) – World PremierePATCH, Luka Galešić (Croatia, 2026, 13 min.) – World PremierePORKATAL, Sis Gürdal (Türkiye, France, United Kingdom, 2026, 16 min.) – World PremiereADGWA-ATA, Zsuzsanna Krief (Hungary, France, 2026, 15 min.) – Regional PremiereCOSMONAUTS, Leo Černic (Slovenia, Italy, 2026, 14 min.) – Regional PremiereGO OUTSIDE AND HUG A TREE, Irina Alexiu (Romania, 2026, 14 min.) – Regional PremiereMOTHERHOOD, Anca Damian (Romania, Belgium, France, 2026, 18 min.) – Regional PremiereNOBODY SAID ANYTHING, Tamara Todorović (Serbia, France, Slovenia, Croatia, 2026, 15 min.) – Regional Premiere
	

	Competition Program – Student Short FilmHYENA, Fanny Rösch (Austria, 2026, 23 min.) – World PremiereNEIGHBOR, Dan Grabnar (Serbia, 2026, 17 min.) – World PremiereSISTERS, Sara Ećimović (Serbia, 2026, 21 min.) – World PremiereTHE GO-BETWEEN, Emir Solaković (Bosnia and Herzegovina, 2026, 7 min.) – World PremiereUNWELLNESS, Réka Pinczés (Hungary, 2026, 21 min.) – World PremiereWATER WALLS, Bianka Szelestey (Hungary, 2026, 34 min.) – World PremiereTHE FUTURE IS IN YOUR HANDS, BUT WHY NOT LEND IT TO THE SPECIALISTS?, Alexandra Schinteie (Romania, 2026, 18 min.) – International PremiereA. WANTS OUT (ON NEUTRALITY), Zorah Berghammer (Austria, 2026, 18 min.) – Regional PremiereBONEFUZZ, Márk László (Hungary, 2026, 10 min.) – Regional PremiereOVER THE THRESHOLD, Tara Gajović (Serbia, 2026, 15 min.) – Regional PremiereRABBIT DON’T RUN, Maximilian Conway, Elisabeth Jakob (Austria, 2026, 30 min.) – Regional Premiere

title: Where to Find a Commonwealth Games 2026 Livestream in the U.S. to Watch the Event for Free
description:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Some of t
body:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Some of the best summer athletes in the world are heading to Scotland for the 2026 Commonwealth Games.

	A quadrennial event dating back to 1930, the Commonwealth Games unites thousands of athletes from more than 70 nations across the British Commonwealth — I.e. countries that are currently or were formerly part of the British Empire. So while countries like Canada, Australia, England, New Zealand, South Africa and Jamaica will all be participating, there will not be a U.S. contingent at the Games.


	This year’s Commonwealth Games run from July 23 to August 2 in Glasgow, Scotland, with events airing live and as nightly highlights on channels like 7Plus in Australia and Channel 5 in the UK.

	North American viewers can watch the Games for free on CBC, Canada’s national public broadcaster, via a virtual private network like ExpressVPN or NordVPN (NordVPN excludes a 30-day money back guarantee). Your VPN lets you set your location to Canada to stream the CBC’s coverage of the 2026 Commonwealth Games online from anywhere in the world.

	Or set your location to Australia or the United Kingdom to stream the 7Plus and Channel 5 Commonwealth Games feed online.
	
		How to Watch the 2026 Commonwealth Games From the U.S.	

	You can also watch the Commonwealth Games in the U.S. through beIN SPORTS, the Qatar-based sports media group that is now available in America on streaming services like Fubo and Sling. 

	Fubo offers beIN SPORTS on its own for just $5.99/month, or as part of the Fubo Core package, which gets you 200 live TV channels to stream online for $63.99/month (regularly $88.99 monthly).


	Don’t have Fubo? Test drive the service with a five-day free trial here and use it to stream the Commonwealth Games in the U.S. for free. Fubo’s plans include free DVR so you can record the events to watch back on-demand whenever you want.

	The cheapest way to get beIN SPORTS on Sling, meantime, is to sign up for the streamer’s Sling Orange base plan (from $45.99/month) and then add the Sports Extra package for an additional $11 a month. You’ll get more than 40 live TV channels in total that you can stream online.

	Sling Orange includes 50 hours of free cloud DVR in addition to an on-demand library of free TV shows and movies that you can stream online.
	
		Commonwealth Games 2026 Sports	

	Unlike previous Commonwealth Games, which have featured more than 30 different summer sports, this year’s event will include just 10 sports, the lowest number of events offered at the Games since 1994. Organizers say rising costs, lack of venues and viewership decline have led to the removal of sports like field hockey, diving, rugby sevens and cricket. 

	Still, there’s plenty of big names to watch for in the remaining sports, which include athletics, swimming, artistic gymnastics, boxing and cycling. Paris 2024 Olympic swimming champions Kaylee McKeown from Australia and Adam Peaty from England, are expected to compete, as is Canada’s gold-medal winning 4×100 meter men’s relay team in track and field.

	The track event will also see Scottish middle-distance runner Josh Kerr, who just broke the 27-year world record in the mile, and Jamaican superstar Shericka Jackson, a two-time World Champion who holds the second-fastest time ever recorded in the women’s 200m events.

	Reigning Olympic pommel horse champion Rhys McClenaghan from Northern Ireland will compete in the men’s gymnastics event, where he’ll face off against Cyprus’ Marios Georgiou, who is a former European All-Around champion and a three-time Olympian.

	This year’s Commonwealth Games kicks off with an Opening Ceremony featuring British singer-songwriter Tom Walker (of “Leave the Light On” fame), along with Scottish singer-songwriter, KT Tunstall and other local artists. The last edition of the Commonwealth Games was held in 2022 in Birmingham, where rockers Duran Duran performed at the Opening Ceremony.

schema.headline: Where to Find a Commonwealth Games 2026 Livestream in the U.S. to Watch the Event for Free

title: ‘The Wild Robot’ Character Rigging Studio STIM Sets Canary Islands Operation, Planning to Create More Than 100 Jobs
description: French animation and visual effects  company STIM – whose credits include “The Wild Robot,” “The Garfield Movie” and “Coyote vs. Acme” – is opening
body:
French animation and visual effects  company STIM – whose credits include “The Wild Robot,” “The Garfield Movie” and “Coyote vs. Acme” – is opening a production center in Spain’s Canary Islands, with plans to create more than 100 jobs as it expands its European operations.

	STIM Tenerife is set to begin operating in August and expects to employ 60 people by the end of 2026. Recruitment is already underway for character modelers, set and prop modelers, look-development artists and supervisors, among other high-skilled positions.
	

	The company has chosen to establish the operation within the Canary Islands Special Zone, or ZEC, a low-tax regime for qualifying companies operating in the Spanish archipelago.


	“The opening of STIM Tenerife in the Canary Islands marks a strategic stage in STIM’s evolution,” said STIM Tenerife CEO Amélie Houpline. “We have found an environment here that brings together talent, strong development potential and a dynamic creative community.”

	The new base, she added, will allow STIM to increase its production capacity “without compromising the artistic and technical excellence that defines our identity.”

	Founded by Félix Ferrand, Jordan Soler and Benoit Gielly, STIM specializes in 3D animation, VFX and digital production for film, TV and advertising. With operations in France and Belgium, it has evolved from a character-focused studio into a company capable of handling projects from design and pre-production through to final delivery.

	STIM collaborated with DreamWorks Animation on the facial and body rigging of several characters for “The Wild Robot.” Its other credits include the Sony release “The Garfield Movie,” Warner Bros.’ “Coyote vs. Acme,” FX’s Emmy-winning “Shōgun” and the Annecy-selected Franco-Belgian toon feature “Girl in the Clouds.”
	



	

					‘Coyote vs. Acme’
									“Coyote vs. Acme”
	



	The Tenerife operation will expand the company’s production capacity while supporting financing structures spanning France, Belgium and the Canary Islands. STIM said that footprint could give producers additional options for assembling European animation and VFX financing.

	“Our ambition is not limited to producing international projects from the islands,” Houpline said. “We want to build a studio with a long-term future, generate highly qualified employment and contribute to the development of the Canary Islands ecosystem.”

	STIM’s arrival comes as the archipelago continues to build its animation, VFX and digital-arts sector. According to figures provided by the ZEC, the Canary Islands now host more than 30 specialist companies and close to 2,000 professionals, up from a comparatively small industry less than a decade ago.



	

					ZEC President Pablo Hernández
									ZEC President Pablo Hernández
	



	Companies operating in the islands include Fortiche, the studio behind “Arcane,” as well as Atlantis Animation, 3 Doubles Producciones, Tomavision, Gigglebug, Amuse Studios and Ánima Kitchent.

	Recent expansion has also included Surfing Giant Studios’ Tenerife operation and DNEG’s investment in Ánima Kitchent, backed by the Spanish government’s SETT, as part of a broader effort to scale the sector.

	“A few years ago, there was practically no animation industry in the Canary Islands,” said ZEC President Pablo Hernández. “Today, we have dozens of studios, close to 2,000 professionals and projects for some of the world’s largest entertainment companies.”

	STIM’s arrival, he added, shows that the archipelago “has ceased to be a promise and has become an established reality within the international animation industry.”

	Hernández said the significance of the latest investments goes beyond attracting individual companies or productions.

	“We are seeing the creation of a genuine industrial ecosystem, with local talent, specialist training and companies capable of leading global productions,” he said. “We have moved from attracting specific projects to attracting permanent centers for decision-making, production and creative development.”

	The next phase, according to Hernández, will involve strengthening local intellectual property, developing original content and attracting larger-scale international investment.
	

	Industry projections suggest the Canary Islands could reach between 3,000 and 4,000 specialized animation and digital-arts jobs over the coming years.

schema.headline: ‘The Wild Robot’ Character Rigging Studio STIM Sets Canary Islands Operation, Planning to Create More Than 100 Jobs

title: Daniel Wu on ‘Cold War 1994,’ ‘Twilight of the Warriors’ and Coming Home to Hong Kong: ‘The Craft Still Challenges Me’
description: Daniel Wu received the Best from the East Award at the New York Asian Film Festival, capping a stretch that has put him back at the center of Hong K
body:
Daniel Wu received the Best from the East Award at the New York Asian Film Festival, capping a stretch that has put him back at the center of Hong Kong’s two biggest franchises: “Cold War 1994” and the “Twilight of the Warriors” series.

	“Cold War 1994” is the third installment in the “Cold War” series, a prequel written and directed by Longman Leung and produced by Bill Kong and Ivy Ho, set in the run-up to Hong Kong’s 1997 handover. The first two films, released in 2012 and 2016, were the highest-grossing Hong Kong productions of their respective years. In the new film, Wu plays Peter Choi opposite Terrance Lau’s M.B. Lee, a character previously portrayed as a full-blown villain by Chang Kuo-chu in “Cold War 2.”
	

	Wu tells Variety he never consciously stepped away from Hong Kong film. The pandemic simply thinned out a schedule that had settled into a rhythm of one American project and one Asian project a year. When “Cold War” came along, the pull was the production itself.


	“It’s going to be a quality production, the scale of it, the script,” Wu says of what drew him back, pointing also to the chance to work alongside three generations of Hong Kong actors, including Lau, whom he singles out as representative of the industry’s new generation.

	The film folds a police procedural into a much larger political canvas, tracking the British colonial government, the Hong Kong police, a powerful business dynasty and the criminal underworld as the territory changes hands. Wu, who was 22 at the time of the actual handover, says the setting let the production dig into a period of corruption and upheaval he lived through but never fully understood until now.
	

	Playing a younger version of a character already established as an antagonist gave Wu a different kind of challenge than he’s used to. He calls the role the most morally ambiguous he’s taken on, since it required tracing how a man who becomes an “evil mastermind” gets there gradually, through a series of compromised choices, rather than starting out that way.

	Returning to a Hong Kong set after several years away felt less like a job and more like a reunion, Wu says. He recalls arriving for his costume fitting and recognizing nearly everyone in the room, a small-industry familiarity he doesn’t get on American productions, where he’s lucky to know one or two people on a given crew. Sitting down on set with his rice box for lunch, eating char siu fan alongside people he’d worked with for decades, brought back what he calls a genuinely warm feeling. He credits Hong Kong crews with a particular work ethic: “efficient, fast, and no bullshit,” a directness he links to a broader Hong Kong attitude of simply getting things done.

	Wu spent much of his “Cold War 1994” press tour in Hong Kong wearing a hat that, it turns out, was hiding a new haircut for his role in “Twilight of the Warriors: The Final Chapter,” the sequel to director Soi Cheang’s “Twilight of the Warriors: Walled In.” That 2024 film, adapted from Yu Yi’s novel “City of Darkness” and the manhua of the same name by Andy Seto, became Hong Kong’s second-biggest commercial hit ever and swept the Hong Kong Film Awards. Where “Walled In” was set in the 1980s, the new installment moves the story to 1993, the year Kowloon’s Walled City was demolished.

	Wu, who studied the Walled City’s architecture in school, calls the setting a rare chance to build a mainstream action franchise around a genuinely singular piece of Hong Kong urban history. He sees both projects as part of a broader shift in how Hong Kong cinema treats its own past, using the 1990s, a decade he says filmmakers “glossed over” while living through it, as material for reflection rather than backdrop.

	That shift, Wu says, is happening at a moment when Hong Kong productions can draw on newer digital tools while still leaning on the practical, in-camera stunt work the industry built its reputation on, recalling a time when performers “threw themselves off the top of a building and just did it for real in camera.” He pushes back on the long-running narrative that Hong Kong film is dying, arguing instead that it’s in something closer to a renaissance, even with a fraction of the annual output it had in the 1980s and ’90s. “There’s a lot of interesting films being made, whether big scale or small scale, a lot of great performances, a lot of great scripts being written,” he says.
	

	The kinds of roles Wu is offered in Hong Kong have shifted too, from the younger leading-man parts that defined much of his career to more mature, fatherly characters, a change he credits partly to age and partly to the character work he’s done in the U.S., where his credits have included the AMC martial-arts series “Into the Badlands,” HBO’s “Westworld,” the film “Tomb Raider” and the animated series “American Born Chinese.” He says that kind of opportunity has thinned out generally, arguing Hollywood has moved past a period of genuinely interesting roles for Asian American actors and back toward “crumbs” or stereotypical parts. He’s more pointed about the ceiling he still sees in the industry, where he says opportunities for Asian American actors, and especially Asian American males, remain narrow.

	Moving between the two industries has left Wu with what he describes as an unusual sense of identity, neither fully of one world nor the other, but both at once. He notes that his experience differs from most Asian American peers, many of whom have never topped a call sheet, while his own career has run the opposite path: character roles in the U.S., leading roles in Asia. He also points to a structural difference between the two systems: Asian productions tend to run through a single director’s vision, while larger American productions are often shaped by committee, with multiple producers weighing in, leaving him unsure at times whose direction to follow.

	Looking back over 25 years, Wu points to his collaboration with director Derek Yee, spanning six films including “One Nite in Mongkok,” “Drink-Drank-Drunk,” “Protégé” and “Triple Tap,” as the turning point that pulled him away from the romantic leads he was initially cast in and toward the darker, more complex characters he prefers. He likens the partnership to Robert De Niro’s with Martin Scorsese, a shorthand so complete that he’d commit to a Yee project without reading the script. “That put my career in a direction that I wanted it to go,” he says.

	Wu is now developing two scripts for the American market and one for the mainland Chinese market, a deliberate move toward more proactive control over his own projects, with an eye toward eventually returning to directing, nearly two decades after his 2007 feature debut, “Heavenly Kings.” Whatever comes next, he says the appeal hasn’t changed. Whether it’s a script’s demands or the actors around him, every project still teaches him something new. “I would say the craft, the craft still challenges me,” Wu says.

schema.headline: Daniel Wu on ‘Cold War 1994,’ ‘Twilight of the Warriors’ and Coming Home to Hong Kong: ‘The Craft Still Challenges Me’

title: Courtney Stodden Is Fighting for Something Bigger Than Jason Alexander’s Apology
description:
It took fourteen and a half years, but Jason Alexander finally apologized.

	On Wednesday, the “Seinfeld” actor issued a public apology to Courtney
body:
It took fourteen and a half years, but Jason Alexander finally apologized.

	On Wednesday, the “Seinfeld” actor issued a public apology to Courtney Stodden for a “comedy” sketch the two filmed together wherein Alexander, who was 52 at the time, fondled a 17-year-old Stodden’s breasts with a cell phone and said that when she turned 18, he would take her behind the couch and have his way with her.


	“Looking back at the comedy sketch in which Ms. Stodden and I participated in 2012, I completely agree that it was inappropriate and I truly regret it,” Alexander said in a statement to Variety. “But more importantly, I am deeply sorry for any harm or distress it has caused Ms. Stodden. I offer her my sincerest apologies.”
	

	The sketch, a parody of “Dr. Phil” dubbed “The Donny Clay Show” with Alexander playing a creepy talk-show host, was released in Jan. 2012 on the Funny or Die website and its YouTube channel (it has since been taken down). Stodden is featured in the video alongside Doug Hutchison, a journeyman actor who notoriously married her in 2011 when he was 51 and she only 16. At the time, the media mainly treated Stodden as a punchline while failing to hold Hutchison accountable for his predation. Alexander gave his apology after Stodden, who is now 31 and divorced from Hutchison, made an Instagram post recounting how “alone” and exploited she felt making that video.


	Stodden tells Variety she was pleasantly surprised that Alexander took responsibility for his role in the disturbing sketch, which he also co-wrote, but could take it a step further. “I’m very taken aback that an adult was actually able to take accountability because I haven’t heard a lot of adults take accountability,” Stodden explains. “When I first got word that he publicly apologized I was grateful because I think that’s the first step in change. At the same time, I think it would be even better if he put that into action, so I would urge Jason Alexander to support AB 1267 and support what’s happening in California or even donate to Unchained at Last.”
	

	Recently, Stodden has been working with the group Unchained at Last, the only nonprofit in the U.S. dedicated to ending child marriage, to convince California to pass AB 1267, a bill that would effectively ban child marriage by making the legal minimum age to wed 18 years old.

	“It’s really frightening that there’s no minimum age for someone to get married in California,” Stodden asserts. “There are only two other states that allow this, Mississippi and New Mexico, and to think that California allows this is shocking.”

	As for the Funny or Die sketch, Stodden says it was only years after appearing in the video that she realized just how foul it was. The realization came as she was watching “Pretty Woman” for the first time and arrived at the scene where Jason Alexander’s villain assaults Julia Roberts’ prostitute in a hotel suite.

	“I was in shock,” she says of watching the “Pretty Woman” scene. “It felt very close to home, and like he pulled from ‘Pretty Woman’ and thought it would be funny to recreate that with a child. I didn’t know he was going to be feeling me up on set.”

	Stodden continues, “I was very manipulated. Even if I didn’t look like a child, or what a ‘victim’ should look like, there was a child buried under there. I really just trusted the adults around me, and this is the effect of that. Looking back on it, it’s really damaging. This isn’t about revisiting my past but changing the experiences for minors going forward so they don’t have to experience what I had to experience.”


	Even though she’s received public apologies from Chrissy Teigen for cyberbullying her, and now Jason Alexander, Stodden never received any sort of mea culpa from Dr. Drew Pinsky who, when she was only 16 years old, performed an ultrasound on Stodden’s breasts on his show “Lifechangers” to prove they were real.

	“Dr. Drew actually doubled down and had his wife sarcastically invite me on his podcast to further exploit me,” Stodden recalls. “Getting an apology from an actor and not a doctor is kind of diabolical.”

	Stodden, along with a group of other survivors affiliated with Unchained at Last, will be visiting the California State Capitol again next month to speak to legislators about ending child marriage in the state she calls home. She’s also focused on finishing her memoir and may be participating in a docuseries chronicling her fight to end child marriage.
	

	“At the end of the day, I don’t want to be remembered for what happened to me,” Stodden states. “I want to be remembered for helping make sure that this doesn’t happen again to another child.”

schema.headline: Courtney Stodden Is Fighting for Something Bigger Than Jason Alexander’s Apology

title: Yeon Sang-ho Returns to ‘Parasyte’ Universe With ‘Tamiya,’ Starring Shioli Kutsuna, at Netflix
description: Yeon Sang-ho is returning to the “Parasyte” universe with “Parasyte: Tamiya,” a new live-action series starring Shioli Kutsuna for Netflix, set to s
body:
Yeon Sang-ho is returning to the “Parasyte” universe with “Parasyte: Tamiya,” a new live-action series starring Shioli Kutsuna for Netflix, set to stream in 2027.

	The series marks another collaboration between Netflix and Yeon (“Hellbound,” “Parasyte: The Grey”), who developed “Parasyte: Tamiya” and co-wrote the script with Ryu Yong-jae. Wowpoint, the production company behind Yeon’s “Human Vapor” and “Parasyte: The Grey,” is producing. Kakimoto Kensaku (“Glass Heart”) and Nakagawa Kazuhiro (“Double,” “Bullet Train Explosion”) are directing.
	

	“Parasyte: The Grey” spent four weeks on Netflix’s Global Top 10 Non-English TV list and reached No. 1 in 34 countries following its 2024 release.


	In the original manga, Ryoko Tamiya is a parasite who takes over the body of a high school teacher, initially violent and indiscriminate before gradually learning from her human surroundings. Witnessing a parasite and human sharing a single body leads her to wonder whether coexistence might offer her species a better chance of survival than conflict. “Parasyte: Tamiya” tells the story from her perspective, tracking the original human, the parasite who inhabits her and the creature caught between both worlds.

	Kutsuna (“Sanctuary,” “Deadpool 2,” “Gundam”) plays Ryoko Tamiya across three registers: the original human, the parasite after it takes over and the parasite as it struggles to interact with humanity.

	Yeon said, “Her struggles over the nature of her own existence are what elevate the original ‘Parasyte’ from a simple hero-based manga aimed at kids to something deeper. I think the anguish she experiences is the essence of ‘Parasyte.'”
	

	Yeon was also asked about his choice of lead. “I’m sure that was a big challenge for Kutsuna as an actor,” he said. “I think she did a wonderful job of portraying the process of a non-human creature coming to understand both humanity and its own existence.”

	On the choice of directors, Yeon said, “I chose these two directors precisely because they have such different styles, and I had high hopes for a collaboration between them.”

	Kutsuna said, “Ryoko Tamiya is the most challenging role I have ever played in my acting career. This series was brought to life through the dedication of the entire cast and crew, who supported one another through every challenge along the way.”

	Netflix Japan’s Sato Yoshihiro, manager of live action creative, added, “‘What makes a human?’ ‘Can we coexist with beings that are different from us?’ These questions posed by ‘Parasyte’ more than 30 years ago feel much more pressing for our modern society that has placed non-sentient AI at all of our fingertips.”

	The “Parasyte” manga, created by Hitoshi Iwaaki and published by Kodansha, has previously been adapted into a film series, an anime and the Korean drama “Parasyte: The Grey.”

schema.headline: Yeon Sang-ho Returns to ‘Parasyte’ Universe With ‘Tamiya,’ Starring Shioli Kutsuna, at Netflix

title: Shaken, Soaked and Seasick: What It’s Like to Survive ‘The Odyssey’ in 4DX
description: There’s no question that seeing Christopher Nolan’s epic “The Odyssey” in 70mm and Imax – or both – is an expansive and breathtaking experience. But
body:
There’s no question that seeing Christopher Nolan’s epic “The Odyssey” in 70mm and Imax – or both – is an expansive and breathtaking experience. But for cinephiles who have already checked off those formats on their list, what’s it like to watch the Matt Damon-starring, three-hour blockbuster in 4DX?

	The seat-rumbling, water-spritzing technology, available in nearly 800 movie theaters around the world, promises a “fully immersive cinematic experience that makes you feel like you’re in the movie.” But does it really? Three intrepid Variety writers – two who had seen the movie before and one first-timer – set out on an odyssey to find out. The three sailed into Regal’s L.A. Live cinemas and were fortunately better-provisioned than Damon and his exhausted crew. Tanya stockpiled mozzarella sticks and Gatorade, while Pat smuggled in a peanut butter protein bar and a bottle of water for the three-hour tour.
	


	Pat was about to put in some eyedrops to prep for the long running time when the moviegoers suddenly embarked on the first stop of the epic journey: the trailer for 4DX. The seats heaved and jiggled, we were spritzed with water, we were shaken and stirred and primed for equally bouncy trailers for “Spider-Man: Brand New Day” and “The Fast and the Furious” rerelease. The onslaught during the pitch for enlisting in the Navy was particularly jarring.

	Then the film began. Read on to see who survived the hero’s journey.



	Pat 

	The main selling points for 4DX are shaking seats and spraying water, with a little wind thrown in. The breeze sometimes felt like the person sitting behind me was ruffling my hair – but since it was my own co-worker, that seemed unlikely. During the many scenes of roiling seas and inevitable shipwrecks, the shaking met the action appropriately. Did it add to the experience? Kind of — we could certainly empathize with how battered and bruised Odysseus and his men must have felt after all those cursed voyages.


	The water was not as bad as I had feared – I had prepared by bringing a hoodie in case of a full Sea World-esque splash zone, but the light spritzing was fairly refreshing. Nonetheless, midway through the film I took advantage of the “turn water off” button to enjoy Nolan’s ambitious filmmaking with fewer distractions. The shaking was particularly effective as the boat was being thrashed around by the whirlpool of Charybdis, one of several moments when it really matched the action onscreen. But did we really need to heave up and down every time a soldier marched into a castle? Just because you CAN shake the seats doesn’t mean you SHOULD every time there’s movement in the frame.


	Overall, I enjoyed my first 4DX experience though perhaps a different and simpler film would have been a better choice. I might have liked a few more effects – the smell of pigs, or ripening cheese, perhaps. “We were all praying for forgiveness,” Damon intones in his climactic speech. In the case of 4DX filmgoers, it could be said that after three hours we were all praying mostly for our seats to hold still.

	Matt

	I will kick off my thoughts with a caveat — I couldn’t make it through the entire 4DX experience. I can handle pretty much any roller coaster, but three hours of movement… I’m no Odysseus!

	I began getting nauseous about halfway through, and my stomach couldn’t handle the thought of experiencing the crazy water scenes that I knew were coming closer to the end of the film. But from what I was able to witness for myself, the 4DX experience was a fun, unpredictable mixture of movement happening in your seat and things happening elsewhere in the room, mainly some mist that helped set the scene when Odysseus and his men are out at sea. The audience also gets a necessary break from the constant movement during the more dialogue-heavy scenes, to be fair!

	I don’t know if I would do 4DX again, especially considering the price tag compared to my beloved AMC A-List getting me into Imax and Dolby screenings at no extra cost, which both enhance the film with better sound. For “The Odyssey,” though, it was worth taking the journey, although I wouldn’t have shelled out the $36 for any other film this summer. If you’re looking to check off as many premium formats for Christopher Nolan’s epic as possible, I’d recommend watching the film once before you try out 4DX so you don’t spend your first viewing trying to parse through the very dense plot while trying not to get knocked out of your seat.
	

	Tanya

	As a frequent 4DX filmgoer (and someone who had already seen Nolan’s epic), I was fully prepared for an Odyssey-esque journey of my own. I stocked up on snacks to fuel my nearly three-hour voyage, but, much like Odysseus’ experience, eating proved to be a challenge. I attempted to dip my mozzarella stick in its accompanying sauce during what I had perceived to be a scene that did not require any motion, but our chairs suddenly jolted forward, sending my over-priced mozzarella stick to the floor. Sure, there were plenty of calmer moments to come throughout the film, but my fear of the upcoming action was enough for me to opt to stay hungry.

	When I first saw “The Odyssey” in Imax 70mm, I thought to myself “This couldn’t get any better.” Seeing it in 4DX immediately proved me wrong. You’re not just immersed in the film — you’re living in it. You are battling with Odysseus’ men, dodging strikes from the Laestrygonians, breathing in actual smoke during the Battle of Troy, getting hit in the back in the same spots soldiers were pierced with arrows. You’re experiencing everything they went through — both the men of Greek legend and the actors themselves, which gives a newfound appreciation of the work that went into the film.

	Above all, the best part of this experience is the energy it created in the theater. We had lived through the film together. There were subtle frightened gasps (and even shrieks of terror) during action-packed moments, and we collectively let out sighs of relief when those moments were over. I locked eyes with strangers, sharing laughs while we attempted to not fall out of our seats. We were actively in the film, and when Odysseus finally revealed he had returned to Ithaca, the entire theater erupted into applause. It felt like we had made it home too. Moments like these are hard to replicate.

schema.headline: Shaken, Soaked and Seasick: What It’s Like to Survive ‘The Odyssey’ in 4DX

title: Carnival Cruise Passenger's Foot Scratching Videos Go Viral
description:
Carnival Cruise
                          Want Some Toe Jam With Your Fork?!? 🤮
body:
Carnival Cruise
                          Want Some Toe Jam With Your Fork?!? 🤮
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 22, 2026
                            5:55 PM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
        
                    
                  
      
    
            Diarrhea ain't the only risky biz on cruise ships. One Carnival passenger put her grossest foot forward in an absolute horror show -- a plate full o' food, with one bitch of an itch sprinkled on top!!!
      
    
            The now viral videos show a woman flakin' the hell out as she repeatedly scrapes dry skin from the bottom of her bare foot while, apparently, sitting in a dining area in plain view of her fellow cruisers. Talk about an appetite suppressant!
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            It's clear from the video she's scratching and eating at a table with other people ... as she casually attacks the bottom of her foot, and the flakes float to the ground like snow.
      

            Somehow, that's not even the grossest part. Moments later, she appears to dust off her fingers and reach back for food off her plate -- perhaps some pizza crust -- and keeps eating without missing a beat.
      
    
        
                    
                  
      
    
            She even called for backup when her fingers weren't getting the job done ... she leveled up and used a metal fork to scratch harder. Mercifully, the footage ends before we can see if the utensil was put back into food service.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
            Carnival's got a sense of humor about the viral ordeal ... joking on Instagram, "We can confirm that forks are intended for food, not foot care." The cruise line said staff quickly stepped in, spoke with the guest and pulled the fork outta circulation.
      
    
            The company also stressed the incident doesn't reflect its standards, noting that while the video's getting plenty of laughs and horrified reactions online, sanitation is something it takes seriously.
      
    
            Take a cruise ... it's endless fungus!!!
      
      
              
            
              Related articles
            
        
              
          
                          
                Internet Culture
              
                          
                Viral
              
                          
                Culture

schema.headline: Carnival Cruise Passenger's Foot Scratching Videos Go Viral

title: Lena The Plug: Mystery Man Who Filed Fake Divorce Serves Adam22
description:
Lena The Plug
                          Person Behind Fake Divorce Serves Adam22 With Court Docs
body:
Lena The Plug
                          Person Behind Fake Divorce Serves Adam22 With Court Docs
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 23, 2026
                            1:00 AM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
            The person who filed divorce docs claiming to be Lena the Plug apparently isn't done trying to move the case forward … despite Lena stating publicly she wasn't leaving Adam22.
      
    
            On Tuesday, court docs were filed by the person claiming to be Lena. The filing, obtained by TMZ, shows a process server delivered a bunch of court docs to Adam22 on July 15 at his $3 million mansion in Studio City, CA.
      
    
            Adam22 confirms to TMZ he was served with legal papers. He says he wants the police or FBI to intervene in the matter, but hasn't heard back from any agency. 
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            As TMZ first reported, on June 3, a divorce petition was filed in Los Angeles County Superior Court, which claimed Lena was asking for a divorce from Adam22.
      
    
            Lena says she's not the one who filed, though.
      

            The petition stated Lena was asking for primary custody of the 5-year-old she shares with Adam22. The filing also alleged Lena was asking for support, claiming Adam22 only gives her $3K per month to live on.
      
    
        
                    
                  
      
    
            The real Lena told TMZ the divorce was not filed by her.
      
    
            She said she believed it was done by a mystery man who she thinks may be getting catfished by someone pretending to be her.
      
    
      
        
              
    
    
            Lena believes the alleged catfish is telling the man that she is in danger and needs help escaping from Adam22 ... which Lena claims is all nonsense.
      
    
            At the time, Lena said she was hiring a lawyer to have the case dismissed ... but it does not appear she's made any official moves in court yet.
